fact: A 7.1 magnitude earthquake struck Messina at 5:20 a.m. in 1908
A twelve-metre tsunami followed the earthquake and struck the ruins
source: National Geophysical Data Center / NOAA Historical Tsunami Database; also Baratta, M. (1910), 'La catastrofe sismica calabro-messinese'

fact: Approximately eighty thousand people died in the disaster
source: Widely documented; estimates range from 75,000 to over 100,000. USGS and Encyclopedia Britannica cite figures in this range.

fact: Buildings were centuries-old unreinforced stone with no modern building codes
source: Guidoboni, E. & Valensise, G., 'Il peso economico e sociale dei disastri sismici in Italia negli ultimi 150 anni' (2011); also widely documented in accounts of Messina's pre-earthquake construction

fact: Relief efforts were delayed for roughly three days because officials initially refused to believe the scale of the disaster
source: John Dickie, 'Darkest Italy: The Nation and Stereotypes of the Mezzogiorno, 1860–1900' and contemporary press accounts; the Italian government's slow response is widely documented, with the first significant naval relief arriving on December 30, two days after the quake
